en.extract:
The African pitta (Pitta angolensis) is an Afrotropical bird of the family Pittidae. It is a locally common to uncommon species, resident and migratory in the west, and an intra-African migrant between equatorial and southeastern Africa. They are elusive and hard to observe despite their brightly coloured plumage, and their loud, explosive calls are infrequently heard. The plump, somewhat thrush-like birds forage on leaf litter under the canopy of riparian or coastal forest and thickets, or in climax miombo forest. They spend much time during mornings and at dusk scratching in leaf litter or around termitaria, or may stand motionless for long periods. Following rains breeding birds call and display from the mid-canopy.


Taxonomy
The African pitta was described by the French ornithologist Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1816 and given the binomial name Pitta angolensis. Vieillot had introduced the genus Pitta in another book published in the same year.
Three subspecies are recognised:

P. a. pulih Fraser, 1843 – Sierra Leone to west Cameroon
P. a. angolensis Vieillot, 1816 – southwest Cameroon to northwest Angola
P. a. longipennis Reichenow, 1901 – southeast Democratic Republic of the Congo to southwest Tanzania and south to northeast South Africa. Migrates outside the breeding season to Central African Republic, north D.R. Congo and south Kenya.
The closely related green-breasted pitta (Pitta reichenowi) replaces the African pitta in the interior of Africa's tropical rainforests. Together the two species form part of a wide-ranging Old World superspecies, which in relatively recent times colonised Africa from the east.
It has been suggested that the races pulih and angolensis may be conspecific with the green-breasted pitta. The migratory race longipennis may also be a separate species.


Description
The sexes are alike. The crown, face, and ear coverts are solid black, and the throat is pale salmon pink. The broad eyebrow is buff to brownish buff. The flanks, breast, and side of the neck are a mustard yellow, washed olive on the upper breast. Some western birds have the breast very greenish. The wing coverts are deep green and tipped turquoise blue, or black and tipped turquoise and royal blue. The mantle and back are green, and the rump and upper tail coverts pale turquoise blue. The wings are rounded, and the primaries are black with pale and white tips. The bases of the central primaries form a white square, conspicuous in flight or display. The belly and undertail coverts are crimson red, and the legs are pink. Immature birds have a duller plumage with a buffy-pink vent and fawn-coloured throat.


Distribution and habitat
The African pitta is a migratory species to southeastern Africa and the Congo Basin. Its breeding habitat in southeastern Africa is deciduous, lowland riparian forest or thicket with intermittent dense understorey and small sub-canopy glades. On migration however, they may sojourn at any areas of bush or woodland. Fallen dead trees and open branches are favoured perches when performing their peculiar bouncing display. They are more numerous in undisturbed vegetation, and the opening up of the riparian woodland by elephants may reduce their habitat.


Migration
The race P. a. longipennis spends the austral winter in the western Ugandan forests as far north as Budongo, and coastal Kenya as far north as Gedi ruins. A bird found at Minziro Forest in northwestern Tanzania was in heavy moult, suggestive that the area is on the southeastern fringe of the non-breeding range.
They arrive in southern Africa from late October, though mainly in November and early December. They seldom breed north of the Rukwa Valley and Rufiji River in Tanzania, and no further south than central Mozambique. They depart again in February, though occasionally as late as April. Ringing studies in the Pugu hills and Mufindi have confirmed the timing of northwestward migration. Exhausted and perished birds are regularly found during migration, especially November to December and April to June. Southward migrating birds sometimes overshoot when they follow moist tropical fronts (at night), which may account for their vagrancy in the north-eastern Transvaal and the Zimbabwean plateau. Some reverse migration has been noted after the breeding season.


Behaviour


Habits and foraging
The African pitta moves about by quick hops. It forages singly on leaf litter, where it scratches to uncover insects and mollusks. It may flit the tail as it walks, and run or jump to a low branch when alarmed, or fly to a high branch where it hides by crouching. It has a fast and direct flight.
It has various call notes, including a querulous scolding skeeow, noted by Moreau, a short, deep trill followed by a wing-clap, and a sproo note, accompanied by a small jump. A croaking call may be heard during migration, and a guttural alarm note has been recorded.


Breeding

They are probably monogamous, and display for a few weeks after arrival. Displaying birds utter a far-carrying and explosive quoip as they leap from a lateral branch in mid-canopy. At the same time the wings are opened to reveal the white bases to the primary feathers. Pairs may be spaced 150 meters from one another.
The bulky, untidy nest is a dome-shape structure composed of small sticks, grass and dry leaves. The inside is lined with finer twigs, tendrils and some dry leaves. It is placed 2 to 4 metres (6.6 to 13.1 ft) above ground in the fork of a sapling, or in the thorny and leafy branches of Acacia, Ziziphus, Ximenia or Dichrostachys. A projecting lip beside the side entrance is used as a landing platform. Egg-laying takes place from November to December in southeastern Africa, and the birds fall silent once incubation starts. Three to four eggs are laid. They are white or cream in colour, and flecked with grey undermarkings and liver-red to blackish-brown markings near the thicker end. They measure 28 by 23.5 mm (1.10 by 0.93 in). The nestlings are altricial and nidicolous.


Status
A decline has been noted in the coastal forests of Kenya after 1983. Concern has been expressed about lighted buildings in coastal Tanzania, which might pose a collision risk, as the birds are nocturnal migrants. Breeding habitat in the Zambezi valley has been impacted by elephants and agricultural expansion. Habitat loss and fragmentation is ongoing.


Monograph

In 1893, Daniel Giraud Elliot, an American zoologist, published a monograph of the Pitta angolensis.


References

Encyclopedia of Animals: Mammals, Birds, Reptiles and Amphibians. Harold G. Cogger, Edwin Gould, Joseph Forshaw


External links
Xeno-canto: audio recordings of the African pitta

The '''African pitta''' ('''''Pitta angolensis''''') is an [[Afrotropical realm|Afrotropical]] [[bird]] of the family [[Pittidae]]. It is a locally common<ref name=lam>{{cite book |author=Lambert, F. |author2=Woodcock, M. |title=Pittas, broadbills and asities |year=1996 |publisher=Pica Press |location=Robertsbridge, U.K. |isbn=1-873403-24-0}}</ref> to uncommon species, resident and migratory in the west, and an intra-African migrant between equatorial and southeastern Africa.<ref name=eanhs>{{cite book|editor-last=Britton |editor-first=P. L. |title=Birds of East Africa: their habitat, status and distribution |url=https://archive.org/details/breedingseasonso00brow |year=1980 |publisher=East Africa Natural History Society |location=Nairobi |pages=[https://archive.org/details/breedingseasonso00brow/page/112 112]}}</ref> They are elusive and hard to observe despite their brightly coloured plumage,<ref name=tree>{{cite book |last=Tree |first=A. J. |title=Angola Pitta |publisher=South African Bird Atlas Project (SABAP) |url= http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/491.pdf}}</ref> and their loud, explosive calls are infrequently heard. The plump, somewhat thrush-like birds<ref name=zim>{{cite book |last=Zimmerman |first=Dale A. |title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania |year=1999 |publisher= Princeton University Press |isbn=0-691-01022-6 |page=495|display-authors=etal}}</ref> forage on leaf litter under the canopy of riparian or coastal forest and thickets,<ref name=tba/> or in climax [[miombo]] forest. They spend much time during mornings and at dusk scratching in leaf litter or around [[termitaria]],<ref name="rob3">{{cite book |last1=McLachlan |first1=G. R.|last2=Liversidge |first2=R. |title= Roberts birds of South Africa |date=1965 |publisher= John Voelcker Bird Book Fund |location=Cape Town |isbn=0-620-00575-0 |page=246 |edition=  5th impression, revised}}</ref> or may stand motionless for long periods.<ref name=fan>{{cite book|author1=Terry Stevenson |author2= John Fanshawe |title=Birds of East Africa: Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi |year=2004 |publisher=Helm Field Guides |isbn= 0-7136-7347-8 |page=278}}</ref> Following rains<ref name=mps/> breeding birds call and display from the mid-canopy.<ref name=fan/>

==Taxonomy==
The African pitta was described by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in 1816 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Pitta angolensis''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Vieillot | first=Louis Pierre | author-link=Louis Pierre Vieillot | year=1816 | title=Nouveau dictionnaire d'histoire naturelle, appliquée aux arts, à l'agriculture, à l'économie rurale et domestique, à la médecine, etc. | volume=4 | place=Paris | publisher=Deterville |pages=356–357 | doi=10.5962/bhl.title.20211 | language=French | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/21107566 }}</ref> Vieillot had introduced the genus ''Pitta'' in another book published in the same year.<ref>{{cite book | last=Vieillot  | first=Louis Pierre | author-link=Louis Pierre Vieillot | title=Analyse d'une Nouvelle Ornithologie Élémentaire | publisher=Deterville/self | year=1816 | location=Paris | page =42, Num. 137 | language=French| url=https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k9745205x/f48.image }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=NZ wrens, broadbills & pittas | work=World Bird List Version 8.2 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/nz_wrens/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=15 January 2019}}</ref>
* ''P. a. pulih'' [[Louis Fraser|Fraser]], 1843 – Sierra Leone to west Cameroon
* ''P. a. angolensis'' Vieillot, 1816 – southwest Cameroon to northwest Angola
* ''P. a. longipennis'' [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow]], 1901 – southeast Democratic Republic of the Congo to southwest Tanzania and south to northeast South Africa. Migrates outside the breeding season to Central African Republic, north D.R. Congo and south Kenya.

The closely related [[green-breasted pitta]] (''Pitta reichenowi'') replaces the African pitta in the interior of Africa's tropical rainforests. Together the two species form part of a wide-ranging [[Old World]] [[Species complex|superspecies]], which in relatively recent times colonised Africa from the east.<ref name=mps>{{cite book |last=Irwin |first=M. P. S. | pages = 221–222 |title=The Birds of Zimbabwe |year=1981 |publisher=Quest Publishing |location=Salisbury |isbn=086-9251-554}}</ref>

It has been suggested that the races ''pulih'' and ''angolensis'' may be [[conspecific]] with the green-breasted pitta. The migratory race ''longipennis'' may also be a separate species.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Borrow | first1=Nik | last2=Demey | first2=Ron | year=2014 | title=Birds of Western Africa | edition= 2nd | place=London | publisher=Christopher Helm | page=302 | isbn=978-1-4729-0568-0 }}</ref>

==Description==
The sexes are alike.<ref name=fan/> The [[Crown (anatomy)|crown]], face, and ear coverts are solid black, and the throat is pale salmon pink. The broad [[Supercilium|eyebrow]] is buff to brownish buff. The flanks, breast, and side of the neck are a mustard yellow, washed olive on the upper breast. Some western birds have the breast very greenish.<ref name=ibc2>{{cite web |last=Borrow |first=Nik |title=The Internet Bird Collection |url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/photo/african-pitta-pitta-angolensis/possible-hybrid-green-breasted-pitta-breast-more-green-buff-voi |work=Photos: African Pitta (Pitta angolensis) |access-date=3 December 2013}}</ref> The [[wing coverts]] are deep green and tipped turquoise blue, or black and tipped turquoise and royal blue. The mantle and back are green, and the rump and upper tail coverts pale turquoise blue. The wings are rounded, and the primaries are black with pale and white tips. The bases of the central primaries form a white square, conspicuous in flight<ref name=zim/> or display. The belly and undertail coverts are crimson red, and the legs are pink. Immature birds have a duller plumage with a buffy-pink vent<ref name=fan/> and [[fawn (colour)|fawn]]-coloured throat.<ref name="rob3"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The African pitta is a [[bird migration|migratory]] species to southeastern Africa and the [[Congo Basin]]. Its breeding habitat in southeastern Africa is deciduous,<ref name=wt/> lowland riparian forest or thicket<ref name=eanhs/> with intermittent dense understorey and small sub-canopy glades.<ref name=tree/> On migration however, they may sojourn at any areas of bush or woodland. Fallen dead trees and open branches are favoured perches when performing their peculiar bouncing display.<ref name=tree/> They are more numerous in undisturbed vegetation, and the opening up of the riparian woodland by elephants may reduce their habitat.<ref name=tree/>

===Migration===
The race ''P. a. longipennis'' spends the austral winter in the [[Western Region, Uganda|western Ugandan]] forests as far north as [[Budongo Forest|Budongo]], and coastal Kenya as far north as [[Ruins of Gedi|Gedi ruins]].<ref name=eanhs/> A bird found at [[Minziro Forest Reserve|Minziro Forest]] in northwestern Tanzania was in heavy moult, suggestive that the area is on the southeastern fringe of the non-breeding range.<ref name=tba/>

They arrive in southern Africa from late October, though mainly in November and early December.<ref name=eanhs/> They seldom breed north of the [[Rukwa Valley]]<ref name=eanhs/> and [[Rufiji River]] in Tanzania,<ref name=tba/> and no further south than central Mozambique. They depart again in February, though occasionally as late as April.<ref name=tree/><ref name=mps/> Ringing studies in the [[Pugu, Tanzania|Pugu hills]] and [[Mufindi]] have confirmed the timing of northwestward migration.<ref name=tba/> Exhausted and perished birds are regularly found during migration, especially November to December and April to June.<ref name=eanhs/> Southward migrating birds sometimes overshoot when they follow moist tropical fronts (at night), which may account for their vagrancy in the north-eastern Transvaal and the [[Zimbabwean plateau]].<ref name=tree/> Some [[Reverse migration (birds)|reverse migration]] has been noted after the breeding season.<ref name = rob4/>

==Behaviour==
===Habits and foraging===
The African pitta moves about by quick hops.<ref name="rob3"/> It forages singly on leaf litter, where it scratches to uncover insects and mollusks. It may flit the tail as it walks, and run or jump to a low branch when alarmed,<ref name=zim/> or fly to a high branch where it hides by crouching.<ref name="rob3"/> It has a fast and direct flight.<ref name=zim/>

It has various call notes, including a querulous scolding {{Not a typo|''skeeow''}}, noted by [[Reginald Ernest Moreau|Moreau]], a short, deep trill followed by a wing-clap,<ref name=zim/> and a {{Not a typo|''sproo''}} note, accompanied by a small jump.<ref name="rob3"/> A croaking call may be heard during migration,<ref name=fan/> and a guttural alarm note has been recorded.<ref name="rob3"/>

===Breeding===
[[file:Faune de la Sénégambie, vertébrés (1883) (14770887764), Pitta angolensis.jpg|thumb|right|upright=0.5|Egg]]
They are probably monogamous, and display for a few weeks after arrival.<ref name=wt>{{cite book|last=Tarboton |first=Warwick |title=A Guide to the Nests and Eggs of Southern African Birds |year=2001 |publisher=Struik |location=Cape Town |isbn=1-86872-616-9 |page=141}}</ref> Displaying birds utter a far-carrying and explosive {{Not a typo|''quoip''}} as they leap from a lateral branch in mid-canopy.<ref name=fan/> At the same time the wings are opened to reveal the white bases to the primary feathers. Pairs may be spaced 150 meters from one another.<ref name=wt/>

The bulky, untidy nest<ref name=wt/> is a dome-shape structure composed of small sticks, grass and dry leaves.<ref name=tree/><ref name="rob3"/> The inside is lined with finer twigs, tendrils and some dry leaves.<ref name="rob3"/> It is placed {{Convert|2 to 4|m|ft}} above ground in the fork of a sapling, or in the thorny and leafy branches of ''[[Acacia ataxacantha|Acacia]]'', ''[[Ziziphus]]'', ''[[Ximenia]]'' or ''[[Dichrostachys cinerea|Dichrostachys]]''. A projecting lip beside the side entrance is used as a landing platform.<ref name=wt/> Egg-laying takes place from November to December<ref name=tree/><ref name=mps/> in southeastern Africa, and the birds fall silent once incubation starts.<ref name=wt/> Three to four eggs are laid. They are white or cream in colour,<ref name="rob3"/> and flecked with grey undermarkings and liver-red to blackish-brown markings near the thicker end.<ref name=wt/> They measure {{cvt|28|by|23.5|mm}}.<ref name="rob4">{{cite book|last1=McLachlan|first1=G. R.|last2=Liversidge|first2=R.|title=Roberts Birds of South Africa |date=1978|publisher=John Voelcker Bird Book Fund|location=Cape Town|edition= 4th |isbn=0-620-03118-2|page=328}}</ref> The nestlings are [[altricial]] and [[nidicolous]].<ref name=wt/>

==Status==
A decline has been noted in the coastal forests of Kenya after 1983.<ref name=zim/> Concern has been expressed about lighted buildings in coastal Tanzania, which might pose a collision risk, as the birds are nocturnal migrants.<ref name=tba>{{cite web |title=African Pitta |url=http://tanzaniabirds.net/African_birds/pitta_African/African_pitta_TBAmap.JPG |work=Preliminary Map |publisher=Tanzania Bird Atlas |access-date=4 December 2013}}</ref> Breeding habitat in the Zambezi valley has been impacted by elephants<ref name=tree/> and [[agricultural expansion]]. [[Habitat loss]] and [[habitat fragmentation|fragmentation]] is ongoing.<ref name=hoyo>{{cite book|author=del Hoyo, J. |author2=Elliott, A. |author3=Christie, D.|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World, vol. 8: Broadbills to Tapaculos|year=2003|publisher=Lynx Edicions|location=Barcelona}}</ref>

==Monograph==
[[File:Pitta Angolensis by Daniel Giraud Elliot in 1893.jpg|thumb|Pitta Angolensis by Daniel Giraud Elliot in 1893]]
In 1893, [[Daniel Giraud Elliot]], an American zoologist, published a monograph of the ''Pitta angolensis''.<ref name="Annual Report of the Board of Regents of the Smithsonian Institution 1896 p. 185">{{cite book | title=Annual Report of the Board of Regents of the Smithsonian Institution | publisher=The Institution | series=1917/18-1963/64: Publication / Smithsonian Institution | year=1896 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WKIKND2xNSAC&pg=PA185 | access-date=10 December 2019 | page=185}}</ref>

==References==
{{Reflist}}
*Encyclopedia of Animals: Mammals, Birds, Reptiles and Amphibians. Harold G. Cogger, Edwin Gould, Joseph Forshaw

==External links==
*[https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Pitta-angolensis Xeno-canto: audio recordings of the African pitta]

zh.extract:
非洲八色鸫（学名：Pitta angolensis），是八色鸫科八色鸫属的一种全面迁徙的候鸟，其种加词“angolensis”意为“安哥拉的”。分布于津巴布韦、加蓬、埃塞俄比亚、赤道几内亚、赞比亚、科特迪瓦、安哥拉、多哥、南非、卢旺达、喀麦隆、加纳、中非共和国、尼日利亚、利比里亚、肯尼亚、坦桑尼亚、布隆迪、刚果民主共和国、刚果、几内亚、马拉维、塞拉利昂、乌干达和莫桑比克。全球活动范围约为1,150,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
非洲八色鸫的平均体重约为68.1克。栖息地包括灌木为主的湿地、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、亚热带或热带的旱林、亚热带或热带的（低地）湿润疏灌丛和湿地。


亚种
指名亚种：Pitta angolensis subsp. angolensis
Pitta angolensis subsp. longipennis Reichenow, 1901
Pitta angolensis subsp. pulih Fraser, 1843


参考文献

Pitta angolensis. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27].
